record_kind: HELD NODE — converged in design conversation, deliberately NOT cast. Akien: 'I think we're getting close.' Close is not wrapped, and today produced two lessons about casting rules still being re-derived.
owner: akiendelllinux_cc_0 (CC) — CC gates writes; the triad and the naming are Akien's.
what:
Three distinct primitives, separated by what they are ALLOWED TO DO, plus the nightly loop that composes them.

FILTER — selection. Narrows candidates in a stack: which provider, which model, which factors, to produce an inference call. Discrimination, not compliance. Emits nothing durable; a stack of dozens per call is normal and must stay cheap.

INSPECTOR — compliance. Runs a scripted, or scripted-plus-inference, check and issues FINDINGS. Cites a code it did not write. NEVER ACTS. May red-tag (block) but may never change.

JANITOR — action, scoped to the unowned commons. Processes findings, resolves the ones inside the space it owns, and checks them off on the findings task. Raises everything else to its owner. Never reaches into another owner's space.
why_three_and_not_one: They differ in AUTHORITY, which is the only axis that matters under Law 6. A filter decides nothing outside its call. An inspector may STOP work but never change it. A janitor may change things, but only in a space nobody else owns. Collapsing any two of them re-creates a supervisor — the thing Cairn has no room for, because enforcement here IS gated ownership and there is no enforcer.
the_inspector_metaphor_grounded:
Akien: 'the image in my head was an electrical code inspector.' Grounded literally, because a metaphor cast into code without its literal reading is how the rack became a scheduler (2026-07-18):

1. AN INSPECTOR CITES A CODE THEY DID NOT WRITE. A real inspector does not say 'this looks unsafe', they say 'this violates 210.8(A)(7)'. Their authority comes from the code; an inspector who invents a rule is out of line. THIS IS THE LOAD-BEARING ONE — it is what makes an inference-backed inspector safe, and it is the entire difference between 'a small model thinks this is smelly' and 'this violates the charter-beside-code rule'. Every finding MUST cite a rule that exists independently of the inspector.
2. RED-TAG IS BLOCK, NOT FIX. Work stops until corrected; the inspector never touches the work. This is exactly the Law 6 line and it permits a blocking pre-push check without the inspector ever holding a write.
3. FINDINGS ARE DATED OBSERVATIONS. 'What I saw on this date, against this code.' Closable, expiring. A VALIDATION anchors an intention permanently; a finding does not, which is why a finding IS NOT a validation (Akien's correction, below).
4. RE-INSPECTION IS OWNER-INITIATED. The owner corrects and calls the inspector back. The inspector does not lurk. CP2's loop-back, pointed the right way.
5. RIGHT OF ENTRY, NOT OWNERSHIP. Broad read scope; the only write is to its own findings.

WHERE THE METAPHOR STRAINS: a real inspector is third-party by law, and that independence is what makes them trustworthy. Ours run inside the system they inspect. The externality has to come from somewhere, and it comes from (1) — the rule is external to the inspector even when the inspector is not external to the system.
the_correction_that_produced_this:
CC proposed 'a filter emits a VALIDATION' as the discriminator. Akien: 'VERY WRONG. A VALIDATION is an INTENTION ANCHOR. A filter is usually part of a stack of similar filters that handle a discrimination process — like matching providers and models and any other factors in order to produce an inference call. If every inference call produced dozens of VALIDATIONS that would be problematic.'

The error was worse than a wrong label: it put filter output on the wrong side of a line the Laws already draw. Law 7 — 'errors are loud at DIAGNOSTIC SURFACES and permanent in RECORDS OF TRUTH.' A provider-matching stack is a diagnostic surface. The cost would have run both ways: drown the truth store, AND degrade VALIDATION into something disposable, corrupting the type for everything that legitimately uses it.

THE PRINCIPLE THAT FALLS OUT, and it generalizes past this case: ONLY AN INTENTION-BEARER CAN ANCHOR A RECORD OF TRUTH. A filter in a stack has no intention of its own — it is a step inside something that does. Durability happens at whatever carries the intention: the gate it is bound to, or the device that ran the stack. This is also why VALIDATIONs are naturally rare — intentions are rare relative to operations, and the ratio is supposed to be lopsided.
the_nightly_loop:
Akien's composition, 2026-07-22: 'janitors can process issues found by inspectors and check them off on the findings task. But then bigger issues need to be bubbled up to us — you and I — like at start of day. So that implies we have a day-end grouping of inspectors running at say midnight local time. And the janitor can reply to some of their findings and check them off. And when we start up in the morning, that just appears on the slate.'

Four stages: (1) a GROUP of inspectors fires at midnight local — a group is a CHECKPOINT, the set that runs at a given seam, exactly like rough-in vs final inspection; (2) each issues findings against cited rules; (3) the janitor resolves what lies in the commons it owns and checks those off, raising the rest to their owners; (4) the RESIDUE surfaces on the slate at start of day.

THIS SATISFIES A FALSIFIER CC WROTE EARLIER THE SAME DAY. notes/overnight-drain.json named the wrong-intent signal: 'if it becomes a nightly digest Akien has to read, it has moved the work rather than done it.' Akien's design answers it directly — what surfaces is the residue AFTER automated action, not the raw list. The janitor is the thing that keeps the digest short.
consistency_check_against_this_morning: The nightly loop is predicted by the ticket/task ruling cast this morning, which is evidence the model is load-bearing rather than decorative. The STANDING REGIME (a midnight inspection group exists) is fabric — a bare machine needs it — so it is a TICKET, cast once. Each NIGHT'S RUN is a TASK: a fresh box does not need last night's findings, so its record is a receipt, not provenance. Akien independently called it a findings 'task' in scare quotes, using the word the morning's ruling had already defined.
open_before_this_can_cast: Does FILTER survive as a named primitive, or was it always two things wearing one word? Inspector cleanly takes the compliance half. The selection half — provider/model matching — may need no primitive at all and may just be the inference domain's internal logic. Build-minimal says do not name it until something outside inference_domain needs it.

Where do inspectors berth? An inspector that checks one component's rule could sit beside it; a system-scale inspector has no single address. This is the partition question again and it waits on notes/held-roots-as-stations-or-shelves.

Inspectors need YIELD, same as probes — one that files nothing for thirty nights is a scheduled re-derivation of a settled answer (Law 1) and should lengthen its horizon or retire. Mechanism is T5.1's tenure, but nothing computes it yet.
The slate's charter changes: it gains a job it did not have. Until today the slate carried session continuity; now it is also the surfacing point for overnight findings residue.
traces_to: Law 6 — the whole triad is an authority partition; collapsing any two re-creates a supervisor.
Law 7 — findings are a diagnostic surface, VALIDATIONs are records of truth; the correction above is this Law applied.
Law 3 — a finding is dated because it is a measurement, not a permanent fact about the thing inspected.
Law 1 — inspector yield: a check that never finds anything is the resolver spent on the settled, on a schedule, forever.
provenance: Converged 2026-07-22 across one design conversation that began with two live UU cron jobs found in a sweep for unowned defaults. Path: cron debris -> 'we should not leave artifacts we can't trace' -> heartbeat + callback instead of cron -> a device for sanitation -> CC pushed back that a cleaning device is a supervisor -> Akien named JANITOR ('this isn't a home, it's a workplace') which supplied the commons-only authority rule -> Akien expanded to filters -> CC's VALIDATION error -> Akien named INSPECTORS and findings. Every sharpening in this record came from a correction, not from agreement.
